turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to implement a time-series logger in Rust using the chrono crate and serde_json::Value, but my struct keeps blowing up memory when I try to store millions of rows. It feels like storing every timestamp as a separate field is just wrong.

The issue is that you're likely using a Row-oriented format which requires redundant null checks and pointer indirection for every column. For time-series data, you should switch to Columnar storage where each metric type like temperature or pressure gets its own dense vector. Instead of serializing the whole row with serde_json::Value, consider encoding the delta values between consecutive timestamps directly into a Vec<i32> since most sensor readings change very little over short intervals.
But wait, what if I just store everything as u64 for nanosecond precision? Isn't that more efficient than storing separate columns or using run-length encoding?

Storing raw nanoseconds ignores the semantic meaning of your data and makes querying impossible without parsing. That's why you need a format like Columnar storage where each column is compressed independently. If you implement delta encoding on your timestamp vectors, you can reduce storage by 60-70% because consecutive readings rarely change much. Combine this with run-length encoding for the actual values to handle long periods of constant readings efficiently.
